original: Kalpana Chawla, American astronaut (STS 87) and the first woman of Indian descent to go to space, born in Karnal, East Punjab, India (d. 2003)
Mammoth Cave National Park established with 45,310 acres, including world's largest cave network in Kentucky, US
"SOS" (· · · – – – · · ·) distress signal becomes the worldwide standard for help
Joint reading ofCharles DarwinandAlfred Russel Wallace's papers on evolution to the Linnean Society renders audience awestruck and silent
Balloon covers a record 809 miles over St Louis
Theodore Rooseveltand his Rough Riders charge up San Juan Hill during US's successful assault on the city of Santiago
1st zoo in the United States opens in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
1st x-ray photo of entire body, taken by Arthur Fuchs of Eastman-Kodak Company in Rochester, New York

First of four fatal Jersey Shore shark attacks occurs at Beach Haven when Charles Vansant (25) bleeds to death, challenging previous scientific belief that sharks never harm humans and sparking popular fear over sharks, which persists today
Ice vending machines introduced in Los Angeles 25 lbs, 15 cents
Coca-Cola brings current coke formula to the market
Dave Prowse, English actor (Darth Vader), born in Bristol. England (d. 2020)
Elvis Presley, wearing a tuxedo, sings to a basset hound on TheSteve AllenShow

English architect Michael Ventris says he has solved one of the 20th century's greatest linguistic riddles, by deciphering Linear B in BBC interview. Is an ancient form of Greek on clay tablets from Minoan palace of Knossos.
Louis Blériot, French aviator and 1st man to fly an airplane across English Channel, born in Cambrai, France (d. 1936)
Alice Guy-Blaché, French film director who was the first woman to direct a film (The Fairy of the Cabbages), born in Paris (d. 1968)
Belgian scouting magazine Le Boy Scout Belge publishes "The Adventures of Totor" by cartoonistHergé, his first comic strip series and a precursor to Tintin
Tour de France: Inaugural race begins in Montgeron, a south-eastern suburb of Paris
In China, sunglasses are invented
257cm (100-inch) diameter mirror for telepscope is delivered to Mount Wilson Observatory, in Los Angeles, California
James W. Smith of South Carolina is one of 1st African Americans to attend West Point
Henry Ossian Flipperof Georgia enters West Point Military Academy - later the first African American to graduate
Ignaz Semmelweis, Hungarian physician who discovered the cause of puerperal (childbed) fever and introduced antisepsis into medical practice, born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1865)
Walter Francis White, American civil rights activist who led the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP), born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 1955)
Linda Fagan, American Coast Guard Admiral, and 1st female commander of US military branch (Coast Guard Commandant, 2022-present), born in Columbus, Ohio

"Magna Carta of the Information Age" "A Mathematical Theory of Communication", article of book byClaude Shannonand Warren Weaver laying foundation for information theory, published in "Scientific American"
1st vote on Declaration of Independence for Britain's North American colonies
Slavery abolished in Dutch territories including Sint Maarten, Suriname and the Dutch Antilles, (now marked as Keti Koti Day in Suriname and the Netherlands)
Admiral James C Ross reaches magnetic North Pole
French frigate Medusa wrecked; basis of Géricault's painting "Raft of the Medusa"
MLB Cincinnati Reds' Fred Toney pitches 2 complete games in doubleheader victories over Pittsburgh Pirates, allows 3 hits each game, fewest allowed by any pitcher winning 2 MLB games in one day
New York Yankees outfielderJoe DiMaggio, on way to a record 56, ties Willie Keeler's 44 game MLB hit streak in a 9-2 win over Boston Red Sox at Yankee Stadium
Washington Senators pitching legendWalter Johnsonno-hits Boston Red Sox 1-0 at Fenway Park, the only no-hitter of his illustrious career
Pittsburgh shortstopHonus Wagner, at 42 and 4 months, connects at Cincinnati to become the oldest to hit an inside-the-park HR; Pirates beat Reds, 2-1
In contravention of state law, Robins (Dodgers) play their 1st Sunday MLB game in Brooklyn; owner and manager arrested but escape with a fine; Robins edge Philadelphia, 3-2
114°F (45.6°C), Las Animas, Colorado (state record, tied in 1954; broken in 2019)
Haleakala National Park forms in Hawaii
International Geophysical Year begins; 18-month scientific project lasts until Dec 31, 1958
Algeria votes for independence from France
Burundi & Rwanda gain independence from Belgium (National Days)
First Nations peoples in Canada granted the right to vote in Federal elections, while still retaining their status and treaty rights
US PresidentAndrew Jacksonannounces to Congress bequest by James Smithson of 100,000 gold sovereigns to found Smithsonian Institution in Washington
Battle of Gettysburg begins in Pennsylvania, Union forces halt Confederate GeneralRobert E. Lee's Army of Northern Virginia
GeneralEisenhowervisits front in Normandy
Dutch expedition of Jacques Le Maire and Willem Corneliszoon Schouten returns to Zeeland after two years - discovered Cape Horn route around South America (Le Maire died on return voyage)
Proclamation removes "Dei Gratia" (by the Grace of God) from Canadian coins
Willie Anderson becomes the first 2-time Western Open golf champion, beating fellow Scot Alex Smith by 4 strokes at Michigan's Kent CC
Basketball Association of America (BAA), which later became the National Basketball Association (NBA), holds its inaugural college player draft
Brookfield Zoo opens in Chicago, Illinois
Martha Hughes Cannon, American politician, polygamous wife and women's rights activist (1st US female state senator, Utah 1897-1901), born in  Llandudno, Wales (d. 1932)
Margaret A. Brewer, American soldier and first female General officer in US Marine Corps, born in Durand, Michigan (d. 2013)
Edward Tsang Lu, American physicist and NASA astronaut (STS 84), born in Springfield, Massachusetts
Twyla Tharp, American dancer and Emmy and Tony Award-winning choreographer (Twyla Tharp Dance Troupe), born in Portland, Indiana
Nancy Lieberman, American Basketball Hall of Fame point guard and coach (Olympic silver, 1976), born in Brooklyn, New York
Mike Haynes, American College-Pro Football HOF cornerback (Super Bowl XVIII LA Raiders; First-team All-Pro 1984, 85; 9 x Pro Bowl; NE Patriots), born in Denison, Texas
Victor Willis, American disco singer-songwriter and "cop/naval officer" in the Village People ("In  the Navy", "Y.M.C.A"), born in Dallas, Texas
Fred Schneider, American vocalist and guitarist (B-52's - "Rock Lobster"), born in Newark, New Jersey
Evelyn "Champagne" King, American disco singer ("Shame"; "I'm in Love"), born in The Bronx, New York City
Shirley Hemphill, American stand-up comedienne and actress (Shirley in "What's Happening!!" and "What's Happening Now!!"), born in Asheville, North Carolina (d. 1999)
Wally Amos, American cookie entrepreneur (Famous Amos), television personality, adult literacy advocate, and motivational speaker, born in Tallahassee, Florida (d. 2024)[1]
